sections.heading: Why Tailoring Is Essential
sections.body:
**Tailoring** is the deliberate adaptation of the project management approach, governance, and processes to make them more suitable for the given environment and the work at hand. The PMBOK Guide 7th Edition elevates tailoring as one of the 12 principles of project management, recognizing that no single methodology works optimally for every project.

Projects vary dramatically in size, complexity, risk, industry, regulatory requirements, and team composition. A large infrastructure project with hundreds of stakeholders and strict regulatory compliance requirements demands a very different management approach than a small software development project with a co-located team of five developers.

The dangers of **not tailoring** include:
- **Over-engineering** — applying heavyweight processes to simple projects, creating unnecessary overhead and slowing the team down with excessive documentation, approvals, and meetings
- **Under-engineering** — applying lightweight processes to complex projects, leading to insufficient planning, poor risk management, and inadequate stakeholder communication
- **Methodology mismatch** — forcing a purely predictive approach on a project with high uncertainty, or applying a purely adaptive approach when detailed upfront planning is required by contract or regulation

Tailoring is not about taking shortcuts or ignoring best practices. It is about **thoughtfully selecting and adapting** practices, methods, and artifacts to maximize value while minimizing waste. The project manager — in collaboration with the team and key stakeholders — should consider what practices to use, how to adapt them, and continuously evaluate whether the chosen approach remains effective throughout the project.
keyPoints: Tailoring is a core PMBOK 7th Edition principle — no one-size-fits-all approach exists
Failure to tailor can result in over-engineering, under-engineering, or methodology mismatch
Tailoring is not about shortcuts — it is about thoughtful adaptation to maximize value
The tailoring process should be continuous, not a one-time decision

sections.heading: How to Tailor: Key Considerations
sections.body:
The tailoring process involves evaluating the project context and selecting the appropriate combination of approach, processes, methods, and artifacts. Key factors to consider include:

**1. Project Characteristics**
- **Size and duration** — larger, longer projects typically need more formal planning, governance, and documentation
- **Complexity** — technical complexity, organizational complexity, and the number of interdependencies influence the level of rigor required
- **Criticality** — safety-critical or mission-critical projects demand more stringent quality and risk management processes
- **Uncertainty** — high uncertainty favors adaptive approaches with shorter planning horizons

**2. Team Factors**
- **Team size** — small teams can communicate informally; large teams need structured communication channels
- **Experience level** — experienced teams may need less process guidance; newer teams benefit from more structure
- **Distribution** — co-located teams can use physical artifacts (whiteboards, sticky notes); distributed teams need digital tools and more formal communication practices
- **Access to stakeholders** — if the customer is embedded with the team, agile approaches work well; if access is limited, more upfront planning may be necessary

**3. Organizational Factors**
- **Culture** — organizations with formal cultures expect documentation and governance; innovative cultures may prefer lightweight approaches
- **Governance requirements** — internal policies, stage-gate processes, and approval requirements shape the management approach
- **Existing infrastructure** — available tools, templates, and organizational process assets influence what practices are feasible

**4. External Factors**
- **Regulatory requirements** — regulated industries often mandate specific documentation, testing, and approval processes
- **Contract type** — fixed-price contracts may require more upfront planning; time-and-materials contracts allow more flexibility
- **Industry standards** — certain industries have established frameworks (e.g., ITIL for IT services, GMP for pharmaceuticals) that must be integrated

The tailoring decision should be documented and communicated to the team and stakeholders. Many organizations develop a **tailoring framework** or **methodology selection guide** that helps project managers make consistent, informed choices.
keyPoints: Key tailoring factors: project characteristics, team factors, organizational factors, and external factors
Team size, experience, and distribution significantly influence the appropriate approach
Regulatory requirements and contract type are critical external tailoring considerations
Tailoring decisions should be documented and communicated to the team and stakeholders

sections.heading: Tailoring the Development Approach
sections.body:
One of the most impactful tailoring decisions is selecting the **development approach** — how the project will create and evolve its deliverables. The three primary approaches are:

**Predictive (Waterfall)**
Best suited when:
- Requirements are well-defined and stable
- Technology is proven and well-understood
- Regulatory or contractual obligations require extensive upfront documentation
- The project delivers a single, complete product at the end

Characteristics: sequential phases, comprehensive planning, formal change control, emphasis on documentation

**Adaptive (Agile)**
Best suited when:
- Requirements are uncertain or rapidly evolving
- Early and frequent delivery of value is important
- Close collaboration with stakeholders is possible
- The team is experienced with agile practices

Characteristics: iterative cycles, incremental delivery, continuous feedback, self-organizing teams, minimal but sufficient documentation

**Hybrid**
Best suited when:
- Some project elements are well-defined while others are uncertain
- The organization is transitioning from predictive to adaptive approaches
- Different components require different approaches (e.g., hardware developed predictively, software developed adaptively)
- Regulatory requirements mandate certain predictive elements while the work itself benefits from agility

Hybrid approaches combine elements of both predictive and adaptive methods. For example, a project might use agile for product development sprints while maintaining a predictive schedule and budget framework for stakeholder reporting.

**Continuous Evaluation**
Tailoring does not end at project initiation. Throughout the project, the team should evaluate whether the chosen approach is working and make adjustments as needed. **Retrospectives** in agile projects serve this purpose explicitly, but even predictive projects should periodically assess whether their processes and methods remain appropriate as conditions change.
keyPoints: Predictive suits stable requirements; adaptive suits uncertain or evolving requirements
Hybrid approaches combine predictive and adaptive elements based on project needs
The development approach should be continuously evaluated and adjusted throughout the project
Retrospectives provide a formal mechanism for evaluating and improving the approach

learningObjectives: Explain why tailoring is a core principle in the PMBOK 7th Edition
Identify the key factors that influence tailoring decisions
Compare predictive, adaptive, and hybrid development approaches
Describe how to continuously evaluate and adjust the project management approach

keyTerms.definition: The deliberate adaptation of the project management approach, governance, and processes to make them more suitable for the given project environment.
A development approach in which scope, time, and cost are determined as early as possible. Also known as waterfall or plan-driven.
A development approach that utilizes iterative and incremental cycles, embracing change and delivering working results frequently. Also known as agile.
A development approach that combines elements of both predictive and adaptive methods based on project needs.
A regularly occurring meeting where the team reflects on its processes and practices to identify improvements for future iterations.

sections.heading: Common Project Management Models
sections.body:
**Models** are frameworks that explain how something works — they simplify complex phenomena into understandable representations. In project management, models help project managers understand situations, predict outcomes, and make better decisions.

**Situational Leadership Model (Hersey & Blanchard)**
This model suggests that effective leaders adapt their style based on the **maturity level** (competence and commitment) of their team members:
- **Directing (S1)** — high task, low relationship; used with team members who are new and need clear instructions
- **Coaching (S2)** — high task, high relationship; used with developing members who need guidance and encouragement
- **Supporting (S3)** — low task, high relationship; used with capable members who may lack confidence
- **Delegating (S4)** — low task, low relationship; used with highly competent and committed members who can work independently

**Communication Models**
Effective communication is cited as the most critical success factor in project management. Key models include:
- **Sender-Receiver Model** — a basic model where the sender encodes a message, transmits it through a channel, and the receiver decodes it. **Noise** can distort the message at any point. Feedback confirms understanding.
- **Interactive communication** — real-time, two-way exchange (meetings, calls, instant messaging)
- **Push communication** — sent to recipients who may or may not read it (emails, reports, memos)
- **Pull communication** — placed in a central location for recipients to access when needed (wikis, repositories, dashboards)

**Change Management Models**
Projects frequently introduce organizational change. Useful models include:
- **Kotter's 8-Step Change Model** — create urgency, form a coalition, create a vision, communicate the vision, remove obstacles, create short-term wins, build on the change, anchor change in culture
- **ADKAR Model** — a goal-oriented model focusing on Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, and Reinforcement
- **Lewin's Change Model** — three stages: Unfreeze (prepare for change), Change (implement), Refreeze (stabilize the new state)

These models are not mutually exclusive — project managers often apply multiple models depending on the situation they face.
keyPoints: Situational Leadership adapts the leadership style to the maturity level of team members
Communication types include interactive (real-time), push (sent out), and pull (self-service)
Kotter's 8-Step, ADKAR, and Lewin's models address organizational change management
Project managers should apply multiple models as appropriate to different situations

sections.heading: Project Management Methods
sections.body:
**Methods** are the means of achieving an outcome, output, result, or project deliverable. While models help us understand, methods help us *do*. Modern project management draws on a wide range of methods, and the PMP exam expects candidates to be familiar with the most common ones.

**Scrum**
The most widely used agile framework. Key elements include:
- **Roles**: Product Owner (owns the backlog), Scrum Master (facilitates and removes impediments), Development Team (self-organizing, cross-functional)
- **Events**: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective
- **Artifacts**: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment
- Sprints are time-boxed iterations (typically 1-4 weeks) that produce a potentially shippable product increment

**Kanban**
A visual method for managing work that emphasizes continuous flow:
- **Kanban board** — visualizes work items as they move through stages (e.g., To Do, In Progress, Done)
- **Work-in-Progress (WIP) limits** — restrict the number of items in each stage to prevent overload and improve throughput
- **Pull system** — new work is pulled into the process only when capacity allows
- **Continuous delivery** — no fixed iterations; work flows continuously
- Key metrics: **lead time** (request to delivery), **cycle time** (start to delivery), **throughput** (items completed per time period)

**Lean**
Originating from Toyota's manufacturing system, Lean focuses on maximizing value while minimizing waste. The **seven wastes** in project management include:
- Partially done work
- Extra features (gold-plating)
- Relearning (lack of knowledge transfer)
- Handoffs (unnecessary transfers between people or teams)
- Delays (waiting for decisions, approvals, or resources)
- Task switching (multitasking)
- Defects (rework)

**Extreme Programming (XP)**
An agile method focused on software development excellence through practices like:
- Pair programming, test-driven development (TDD), continuous integration
- Small releases, simple design, refactoring

**Critical Path Method (CPM)**
A predictive scheduling technique that identifies the longest sequence of dependent activities (the **critical path**). Any delay on the critical path directly delays the project finish date. Used to determine project duration and identify schedule flexibility (**float/slack**) on non-critical activities.
keyPoints: Scrum uses time-boxed sprints with defined roles (PO, SM, Dev Team) and ceremonies
Kanban emphasizes continuous flow, WIP limits, and a visual board
Lean focuses on maximizing value by eliminating seven types of waste
The Critical Path Method identifies the longest sequence of dependent activities

sections.heading: Selecting the Right Models and Methods
sections.body:
Choosing the right combination of models and methods is a critical tailoring decision. There is no universally best method — the choice depends on the project context, team capabilities, and organizational environment.

**Decision Factors for Method Selection**

| Factor | Predictive Methods Favored | Adaptive Methods Favored |
|--------|---------------------------|-------------------------|
| Requirements | Well-defined, stable | Uncertain, evolving |
| Customer involvement | Limited availability | High availability |
| Risk tolerance | Low tolerance for change | High tolerance for change |
| Delivery timeline | Single delivery at end | Incremental delivery |
| Team experience | New to self-management | Experienced, self-organizing |
| Regulatory needs | Strict compliance | Flexible compliance |

**Combining Methods**
In practice, projects often combine multiple methods. Common combinations include:
- **Scrum + Kanban (Scrumban)** — using Scrum's structure with Kanban's flow management and WIP limits
- **Agile + Waterfall (Hybrid)** — using agile for development and waterfall for overall project governance
- **Lean + Scrum** — applying Lean waste reduction principles within a Scrum framework

**Method Scaling**
When agile methods need to be applied across large organizations or complex programs, scaling frameworks provide guidance:
- **SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework)** — the most widely adopted enterprise agile framework, organizing work at team, program, and portfolio levels
- **LeSS (Large-Scale Scrum)** — a lightweight framework that extends Scrum to multiple teams
- **Disciplined Agile (DA)** — PMI's own toolkit-based approach that provides a process decision framework

The project manager should evaluate available methods, consider team familiarity and organizational support, and select an approach that balances structure with flexibility. It is equally important to remain open to changing the method if it proves ineffective during execution.
keyPoints: Method selection depends on requirements stability, customer involvement, risk tolerance, and team experience
Methods can be combined (e.g., Scrumban, hybrid agile-waterfall)
SAFe, LeSS, and Disciplined Agile are frameworks for scaling agile across organizations
The chosen method should be evaluated and adjusted throughout the project

learningObjectives: Apply Situational Leadership to different team member maturity levels
Compare Scrum, Kanban, and Lean as project management methods
Explain the Critical Path Method and its role in schedule management
Identify factors that influence the selection and combination of methods

keyTerms.definition: A leadership model by Hersey and Blanchard that suggests leaders should adapt their style (directing, coaching, supporting, delegating) based on the maturity of their team members.
An agile framework that uses time-boxed sprints, defined roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team), and ceremonies to deliver incremental value.
A visual method for managing work flow using a board, WIP limits, and a pull system to optimize throughput and reduce waste.
A philosophy focused on maximizing customer value while minimizing waste, originating from the Toyota Production System.
The longest sequence of dependent activities in a project schedule, which determines the minimum project duration.
A constraint on the number of work items allowed in a given stage of a workflow, used to prevent overload and improve flow.
Scaled Agile Framework — an enterprise-level framework for implementing agile practices across large organizations.
A change management model focusing on five outcomes: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, and Reinforcement.

sections.heading: What Are Artifacts?
sections.body:
**Artifacts** are the tangible outputs, documents, and deliverables produced during project management activities. They serve as communication tools, decision records, planning references, and evidence of work completed. The PMBOK Guide 7th Edition groups artifacts into several categories based on their purpose.

Artifacts are not bureaucratic overhead — when used appropriately, they provide essential value:
- **Alignment** — artifacts like the project charter ensure that stakeholders share a common understanding of the project's purpose, scope, and objectives
- **Transparency** — status reports, dashboards, and backlogs make project information visible and accessible
- **Accountability** — responsibility assignment matrices (RAM/RACI), work assignments, and acceptance criteria define who is responsible for what
- **Knowledge preservation** — lessons learned registers, process documentation, and organizational process assets capture knowledge for future projects
- **Compliance** — in regulated industries, artifacts provide auditable evidence that required processes were followed

The key principle is to create **just enough** documentation to serve these purposes. Every artifact should earn its place — if it does not provide value to the team, stakeholders, or organization, it should be eliminated or simplified. This aligns with the agile value of "working software over comprehensive documentation," though it applies equally to non-software projects.

**Categories of Artifacts**
Artifacts can be broadly categorized as:
- **Strategy artifacts** — business case, project charter, benefits management plan
- **Log and register artifacts** — risk register, issue log, change log, lessons learned register, stakeholder register
- **Plan artifacts** — project management plan and subsidiary plans (scope, schedule, cost, quality, resource, communications, risk, procurement, stakeholder)
- **Hierarchy charts** — work breakdown structure (WBS), organizational breakdown structure (OBS), resource breakdown structure (RBS)
- **Baseline artifacts** — scope baseline, schedule baseline, cost baseline
- **Visual data and information** — dashboards, burndown charts, Kanban boards, control charts
- **Agreements** — contracts, memoranda of understanding, service level agreements
keyPoints: Artifacts are tangible outputs that support alignment, transparency, accountability, and compliance
Create just enough documentation — every artifact should earn its place
Artifact categories include strategy, logs/registers, plans, hierarchy charts, baselines, and visual data
The appropriate level of documentation depends on project context and tailoring decisions

sections.heading: Key Predictive Artifacts
sections.body:
Predictive (waterfall) projects rely on a comprehensive set of artifacts that are created primarily during the planning phase and maintained through change control throughout the project.

**Project Charter**
The project charter is the document that formally authorizes the existence of a project and provides the project manager with the authority to apply organizational resources. It typically includes:
- Project purpose and justification
- Measurable objectives and success criteria
- High-level requirements and deliverables
- Summary milestone schedule and budget
- Key stakeholders and the assigned project manager
- Approval authority (project sponsor)

The charter is created during **project initiation** and remains a reference document throughout the project.

**Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**
The WBS is a **hierarchical decomposition** of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team. It organizes and defines the total scope of the project, with each descending level representing an increasingly detailed definition of the project work.
- The lowest level items are called **work packages** — they are the units of work that can be estimated, scheduled, monitored, and controlled
- The WBS is the foundation for scope management, scheduling, and cost estimation
- The **WBS Dictionary** provides detailed descriptions of each WBS element
- The **100% Rule** states that the WBS must include 100% of the work defined by the project scope — no more, no less

**Project Schedule**
The project schedule includes:
- Activity list with durations and dependencies
- **Gantt charts** for visual representation of the schedule
- **Network diagrams** showing activity sequences and the critical path
- Milestone charts for high-level schedule communication

**Other Key Predictive Artifacts**
- **Requirements Traceability Matrix** — links requirements to their origin and traces them through deliverables and test cases
- **RACI Matrix** — defines roles as Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, or Informed for each deliverable or activity
- **Risk Register** — documents identified risks, their probability, impact, priority, and planned responses
- **Lessons Learned Register** — captures knowledge gained during the project for use in current and future projects
keyPoints: The project charter formally authorizes the project and grants PM authority
The WBS hierarchically decomposes all project scope into manageable work packages
The 100% Rule requires the WBS to capture all project scope — no more, no less
The RACI matrix defines Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed roles

sections.heading: Key Agile Artifacts
sections.body:
Adaptive (agile) projects use a different set of artifacts that emphasize simplicity, visibility, and continuous refinement over comprehensive upfront documentation.

**Product Backlog**
The product backlog is an **ordered list of everything** that is known to be needed in the product. It is the single source of requirements for any changes to be made to the product.
- Owned and prioritized by the **Product Owner**
- Items at the top are refined and ready for the next sprint; items at the bottom may be vaguely defined
- Common formats include **user stories** ("As a [user], I want [feature] so that [benefit]") and **epics** (large user stories that will be broken down)
- The backlog is continuously refined through **backlog refinement** (grooming) sessions where the team estimates effort, clarifies requirements, and breaks down large items

**Sprint Backlog**
The sprint backlog is the set of product backlog items selected for the sprint, plus a plan for delivering the product increment and realizing the sprint goal.
- Owned by the **Development Team**
- Updated daily as the team learns more about the work needed
- Provides a real-time picture of the work the team plans to accomplish during the sprint

**Burndown and Burnup Charts**
- **Burndown chart** — shows the amount of work remaining over time. The ideal line descends from the total story points to zero by the end of the sprint or release.
- **Burnup chart** — shows the amount of work completed over time, along with the total scope line. This makes scope changes visible.
- Both charts provide at-a-glance progress information and help teams identify if they are on track

**Definition of Done (DoD)**
The Definition of Done is a shared understanding of what it means for work to be **complete**. It typically includes criteria such as:
- Code written and peer-reviewed
- Unit tests written and passing
- Integration tests passing
- Documentation updated
- Product Owner acceptance

The DoD ensures quality and consistency across all increments and prevents "almost done" work from accumulating.

**Information Radiators**
Agile teams use highly visible displays — physical or digital — to broadcast project information. Common examples include Kanban boards, burndown charts displayed on monitors, and team velocity charts. The goal is to make information available at a glance without requiring people to seek it out.
keyPoints: The product backlog is the single source of requirements, owned by the Product Owner
User stories follow the format: As a [user], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
Burndown charts show remaining work; burnup charts show completed work and scope changes
The Definition of Done ensures shared understanding of what 'complete' means

learningObjectives: Identify the purpose and categories of project management artifacts
Describe key predictive artifacts including the charter, WBS, and RACI matrix
Explain agile artifacts including the product backlog, sprint backlog, and burndown charts
Apply the principle of creating just enough documentation based on project context

keyTerms.definition: A document that formally authorizes the existence of a project and provides the project manager with authority to apply organizational resources.
A hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team to accomplish project objectives.
An ordered list of all features, requirements, enhancements, and fixes that constitute the changes to be made to the product in future releases.
A short, informal description of a feature told from the perspective of the user, typically following the format: As a [user], I want [feature] so that [benefit].
A graphical representation of work remaining versus time, used to track progress toward a sprint or release goal.
A shared understanding of the criteria that must be met for a product increment to be considered complete and releasable.
A responsibility assignment matrix that defines roles as Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, or Informed for each task or deliverable.
A highly visible physical or digital display of project information, designed to provide at-a-glance status without requiring people to seek it out.
